why_correct: The core issue is coverage bias, not sample size. Hand-curated examples reflect the developer's mental model of the problem, which by definition cannot include unknown edge cases — multi-currency inputs, transliterated text, ambiguous order IDs, emotionally escalated language. An automated eval pipeline with a diverse held-out set surfaces distributional failures before they reach users; ad-hoc manual testing only confirms that the happy path works. Shipping on seven cherry-picked examples means the first signal of failure is a production incident, not a metric on a dashboard.

distractors.A: The failure mode here is treating this as a statistics problem when it's a coverage problem. Doubling to fourteen hand-picked examples doesn't fix selection bias — you're just adding more of the same mental model. A larger manual sample would be meaningful only if drawn randomly from a representative distribution of real traffic, which this team doesn't have yet.
distractors.C: Latency and rate-limit behavior are real operational concerns, but they're infrastructure concerns, not prompt reliability concerns. The question is asking about whether the prompt will produce correct outputs across input diversity — that's a functional correctness problem. Conflating response quality with runtime performance is a category error that distracts from the actual risk.
distractors.D: Fine-tuning is a valid architectural choice for some support workflows, but it's a non-sequitur here. General-purpose prompted models handle support workflows reliably in production every day. Asserting that evaluation is meaningless without fine-tuning inverts the decision order — you evaluate first to determine whether fine-tuning is even necessary.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for eval coverage: manual testing validates that your implementation matches your mental model; automated eval with diverse inputs validates that your mental model matches reality. Any time a stem describes developer-curated examples, the architectural tell is selection bias — the gap between what you thought to test and what users actually send.
cross_domain_note: This is the same coverage gap that breaks RAG retrieval pipelines — if your test queries only cover the document types you indexed intentionally, you won't discover retrieval failures on real query patterns until production traffic exposes them.

why_correct: The failure is the classic single-example iteration loop: fix one failing case, validate that one case, ship. The system prompt is a global instruction that affects the entire routing decision surface — rewriting it to sharpen one category boundary inevitably perturbs adjacent ones. The correct governance pattern is eval-gated deployment: every prompt change must pass aggregate accuracy metrics across all 12 categories before merge, because any structural prompt edit is a global state change, not a local patch.

distractors.A: Model tier swaps don't solve prompt engineering debt — a more capable model will still faithfully execute an ambiguous or contradictory system prompt, just more confidently. Model capability addresses reasoning quality, not the absence of a regression harness. You'd use a larger model if the task were beyond the current model's capability ceiling, which isn't the diagnosis here.
distractors.C: This is the near-miss: few-shot examples do influence behavior locally, but they don't leave other routing behavior 'unchanged' as a guarantee. Claude attends to all context, and few-shot examples that pattern-match one category can shift decision boundaries for semantically adjacent categories. More importantly, this answer doesn't address the root failure — no aggregate eval — it just proposes a different prompt edit without fixing the governance gap.
distractors.D: 10-15 tickets per category sounds like statistical sampling, but it's an arbitrary threshold that sidesteps the actual requirement: a defined pass/fail criterion on aggregate accuracy. Without a target metric, a reviewer can't tell whether 12/15 correct is acceptable or a regression. The governance failure is the absence of a formal eval gate, not the sample size.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: any system prompt change is a global diff, not a targeted patch. The eval surface must match the change surface — 12-category classifier gets a 12-category eval, every time, with a hard acceptance threshold. If your validation scope is narrower than your instruction scope, you have a regression blind spot by construction.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to agentic pipeline governance: the same principle applies when updating tool-use instructions in an orchestration layer — a change to one tool's invocation logic can alter the agent's routing decisions for other tools. Aggregate behavioral evals across the full action space are required before deployment there too.

why_correct: C applies the strangler-fig pattern to eval risk: scope the blast radius to the lowest-liability surface first, build your scoring harness against real production distribution while that slice runs, then use gate-based rollout for the remaining dispute categories. This is the canonical approach when you have an asymmetric risk profile across domains — billing disputes and cancellations carry regulatory and financial liability that spot-testing 20 tickets cannot surface. The eval pipeline isn't a pre-launch ceremony; it's the gating mechanism that makes the other four product lines safe to ship at all.

distractors.A: The failure mode here is confusing observation with evaluation. Production dashboards surface error *rates*, not error *types* — you'll see CSAT drop or escalation spike, but you won't know whether the failure is a prompt regression, a category-specific edge case, or a trust boundary violation until you've already burned user trust. This position is defensible for low-stakes, reversible interactions; it's indefensible when the agent has tool-use authority over account cancellations and refund issuance.
distractors.B: B is tempting because the human review queue looks like a safety net, but it's the 'eval as afterthought' anti-pattern. The queue captures low-confidence flags, not confident-but-wrong responses — and in billing dispute resolution, the dangerous outputs are typically the ones the model delivers with high token-level certainty. You've also launched all five product lines simultaneously, so when the queue overflows (it will), you have no scoped rollback path.
distractors.D: D treats eval coverage as a binary pre-condition rather than a risk-gated process, sacrificing the real production signal you'd get from the safe slice while burning three days on synthetic coverage across dispute categories you don't yet have ground-truth data for.

mental_model: When dispute categories have heterogeneous liability surfaces, always scope the initial launch to the lowest-risk slice and build your eval pipeline against live traffic from that slice. The CCA heuristic: eval gates rollout, not launch — ship the safest scope immediately, let production distribution inform your scoring rubric, then gate expansion on eval results passing threshold.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to the trust boundary and agentic loop safety domain: an agent with write-access to account state (cancellations, refunds) requires progressive capability exposure, not full-scope deployment — the same principle that governs tool-use authorization in multi-step agentic workflows.

why_correct: The failure is a sampling distribution mismatch: fifteen clean, homogeneous invoices cannot cover the edge-case space of real-world document variance — multi-currency splits, payment term structures, vendor-amount attribution ambiguity. The correct pattern when deploying extraction pipelines is a scored eval suite with adversarial and structurally diverse fixtures, because manual spot-testing has no statistical coverage guarantee. Structurally valid but semantically wrong outputs are the canonical failure mode of under-evaluated extraction prompts — they bypass format validators entirely, which is exactly why a scored eval with ground-truth comparison is the only gate that catches them.

distractors.A: Retry policy gaps produce partial outputs or surfaced exceptions — not silent semantic mislabeling. If the symptom were missing fields or 500-class errors logged as successes, A would be in play. The stem explicitly says outputs were structurally valid, which rules out transient network failure as the cause.
distractors.C: XML delimiters are a prompt hygiene concern that reduces ambiguity in field boundary parsing, but the stem says outputs passed format validation. The failure is wrong amounts attributed to wrong vendors — a semantic attribution error, not a structural encoding error. Missing delimiters would more likely cause parse failures, not cross-field value swaps.
distractors.D: Fine-tuning on an invoice corpus is a legitimate optimization for throughput, cost, or latency at scale, but it is not a prerequisite for reliable extraction with a well-engineered prompt. Reaching for fine-tuning before exhausting prompt engineering and eval iteration is the premature optimization anti-pattern — it also adds a months-long feedback loop that doesn't address the actual gap, which was test coverage.

mental_model: Whenever the symptom is 'structurally valid, semantically wrong,' the root cause is almost always eval coverage, not prompt syntax. The CCA heuristic: manual spot-tests on clean samples prove the happy path works, never that edge cases fail gracefully — a scored eval pipeline with diverse, adversarial fixtures is the only architectural gate that catches attribution errors before they reach customers.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to the testing and deployment domain: the same scored eval pipeline that catches extraction edge cases pre-launch is also your regression harness when you rotate model versions or adjust the system prompt — skipping it compounds risk across the entire model lifecycle.

why_correct: The junior engineer's eval pipeline catches distributional failures — edge cases that only appear when you sample broadly across real input variance, not cherry-picked happy paths. Three manually chosen tickets almost certainly share structural similarity and won't expose parsing failures on non-USD currencies, malformed amounts, or ambiguous issue types. A labeled eval set with field-level scoring is the correct pattern here because it converts 'looks right' into a measurable precision/recall signal per output field before you're debugging in prod.

distractors.A: The failure mode here is conflating stylistic consistency with correctness. Rephrasing Claude's outputs to 'sound more consistent' is prompt cosmetics — it does nothing to validate whether extracted field values are accurate. This would be relevant if you were building a customer-facing response generator where tone matters, but structured JSON extraction is graded on field accuracy, not phrasing.
distractors.C: Token usage is observable from the API response object on any sample size — you don't need fifty tickets to cost-forecast, you need maybe five with a p95 buffer. The near-miss quality is that cost forecasting is a real pre-production concern, but fifty tickets buys you eval coverage, not meaningfully better token statistics.
distractors.D: Rate limit behavior is infrastructure validation, not prompt evaluation. That belongs in a load test or integration test against the client's retry/backoff logic, not a prompt eval harness. Conflating the two suggests a misunderstanding of what the eval pipeline is actually measuring.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for eval design: manual spot-checking validates the happy path; a labeled eval set validates the decision boundary. Any time a pipeline has structured outputs with known-correct ground truth, the question isn't whether to build an eval — it's whether your coverage is broad enough to surface the tail failures that production will find for you.
cross_domain_note: This directly gates deployment readiness in agentic or tool-use pipelines — if a structured extraction step feeds downstream function calls, a silent field-level parsing failure (wrong currency code) propagates as a logic error through the entire orchestration chain before anyone notices.

why_correct: Prompt changes are non-local by nature — rephrasing extraction logic for one category reshapes the attention and token probability landscape across the entire instruction set, so regressions anywhere in the distribution are always on the table. The correct acceptance gate is a full suite regression check: the revised prompt must meet or exceed baseline aggregate scores across all 8 categories, not just recover the 3 failing examples. Using targeted failing cases as the only validation signal is the 'whack-a-mole' anti-pattern — it looks like a fix until monitoring catches the regression two days later in production.

distractors.A: A is tempting because it correctly identifies sample size as a problem, but it misframes the solution as 'run more examples per category' rather than 'run the full suite across all categories.' In production, a 20-30 example per-category gate still misses cross-category regressions if you're only sampling within the target category — the architectural failure is scope, not sample count.
distractors.B: B describes a real concern — overfitting your prompt iteration to the observed failure cases — but the issue here isn't confirmation bias inflating perceived improvement on the target category. The actual failure is that no measurement of the other 7 categories happened at all. Holdout splitting is relevant for model training and fine-tuning workflows, not for prompt evaluation against a fixed test suite where the correct move is full-suite re-evaluation.
distractors.D: Temperature as a root cause is a red herring here; structured extraction tasks are typically run at low or zero temperature precisely to minimize output variance, and even if variance existed, the regression pattern is consistent and directional (97% to 81%), not noisy — which rules out stochastic instability as the mechanism.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for prompt iteration: any change to a prompt is a global change to the model's behavior distribution, so the acceptance gate must always be full-suite aggregate metrics, never targeted case confirmation. If your eval scope matches only your fix scope, you're flying blind on regressions.
cross_domain_note: This maps directly to the testing and CI/CD layer of an agentic pipeline — the same principle applies when updating tool-use instructions or system prompt sections: a regression suite covering all tool invocation patterns must pass before shipping, because prompt edits in one section affect parsing and routing behavior across the full orchestration loop.

why_correct: The sliding window with structured summarization pattern directly addresses both failure modes simultaneously: stale-detail hallucination (caused by low-salience early tokens competing for attention) and runaway token costs (caused by O(n²) context growth across turns). By compressing cold history into a structured summary and keeping only recent raw turns, you preserve semantic continuity without paying full token cost for verbatim history. The tightly bounded token budget is the key constraint — it converts unbounded growth into a fixed-cost-per-turn architecture. This is the canonical approach when context growth is the root cause of both accuracy degradation and cost regression.

distractors.A: max_tokens controls output length, not input context capacity — raising it does nothing to address the 8-10 turn input bloat. The failure mode here is confusing response budget with context budget. A would be correct only if the model were truncating its own output mid-classification; that's not what's described.
distractors.C: Upgrading to a larger context window is the 'buy more RAM' anti-pattern — it defers the problem without solving the O(n²) growth dynamic. Costs continue tripling per turn, and attention dilution over long contexts still degrades accuracy on classification tasks. The architectural tell is that token costs have already tripled; throwing a more expensive model at an unbounded growth curve accelerates cost regression rather than controlling it.
distractors.D: Moving the grading rubric to post-processing breaks the classification pipeline's grounding — the model can no longer use rubric constraints to shape its output, so you get raw completions that require a separate reconciliation pass. This trades in-context token cost for post-processing complexity and likely accuracy loss, which is the wrong optimization direction when the rubric is a first-class classification signal.

mental_model: When you see accuracy degradation and cost growth both appearing together after many turns, the root cause is almost always unbounded context accumulation. The CCA heuristic: compress cold state into structured summaries, bound the raw-turn window, and fix the token budget per request — never solve a context growth problem by expanding capacity.
cross_domain_note: This pattern maps directly to the model selection domain: the summarization approach enables you to use a smaller, cheaper model tier consistently rather than being forced into frontier models just to fit raw history, which is a secondary cost lever the team can pull once the architecture is sound.
